title: No SCF data/derivative detection or prevention logic
severity: critical
location: /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-grc/src/agents/repo_documentation/agent.py (full agent)
problem: AGENT.md line 176 states an absolute requirement: 'Never include SCF data or SCF-derived content (verbatim, paraphrase, or structural derivative) in any public-facing document — CC BY-ND 4.0 prohibits redistribution of derivative works.' The current agent implementation contains zero validation logic to detect or prevent SCF content from appearing in generated documentation. The docstring (line 19-22) acknowledges branding compliance checks for independence positioning but says nothing about SCF data filtering. When Phase 1 implements generation, there is no guard rail preventing accidental inclusion of SCF control IDs, domain names, STRM mapping text, or structural derivatives of the SCF framework in executive or practitioner documents.
spec_or_invariant: agents/15-platform-devsecops/repo-documentation/AGENT.md line 176; AGENT.md Failure Modes table line 187 ('SCF-derived content detected in public document' with recovery 'Remove entirely — no paraphrase is safe under CC BY-ND'); docs/charter/PROJECT_CHARTER.md line 177 (SCF license: CC BY-ND 4.0)
proposed_fix: Before Phase 1 implementation, define a set of prohibited SCF-derived patterns: SCF control IDs (G-***, T-***, etc.), SCF domain names (from the 33 editorial domains), STRM relationship type language ('Equal To', 'Subset', etc.), and SCF maturity level terminology. Implement a `_check_scf_content()` method that scans generated content against these patterns and raises a SAPViolationError if any are found. Call this check on all content destined for public documentation (executive, practitioner modes) before returning it. Log all flagged content to lab notebook.

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

1	"""
2	Repo Documentation Agent.
3	
4	PURPOSE:
5	Produces and maintains audience-stratified documentation for the HC-GRC
6	repository. Three modes — executive, practitioner, technical — each written
7	for a distinct audience altitude drawn from the Hipster CISO persona taxonomy.
8	
9	AUDIENCE TAXONOMY:
10	  executive    → P1 (B2B Executive Leader) + P2 (PE/Board/VC)
11	                 BCG/McKinsey altitude: business problem, strategic significance,
12	                 plain language, so-what explicit at every level
13	  practitioner → P3 (CDO/CAIO/CDAIO/CISO)
14	                 Methodological credibility, operational authenticity,
15	                 peer-level depth, tools-not-frameworks
16	  technical    → Developer/contributor
17	                 Implementation-first, dense, code-forward
18	
19	BRANDING COMPLIANCE:
20	  All executive-mode content must pass Branding Compliance Agent review
21	  before commit. Independence positioning and commercial disclosure are
22	  non-negotiable checks.
23	
24	PROTECTED STATUS:
25	  This agent is NOT a PROTECTED research agent per ADR-0015 §77.
26	  Documentation changes do not constitute research design changes.
27	  Agent Evolution may optimize documentation prompts autonomously.
28	
29	See AGENT.md: agents/15-platform-devsecops/repo-documentation/AGENT.md
30	"""
31	
32	from __future__ import annotations
33	
34	from dataclasses import dataclass, field
35	from pathlib import Path
36	from typing import Any, Literal
37	
38	from ...state import HCGRCState
39	from ..base import BaseResearchAgent, SAPViolationError
40	
41	DocumentationMode = Literal["executive", "practitioner", "technical"]
42	
43	# Audience altitude notes injected into generation context by mode
44	_MODE_CONTEXT: dict[DocumentationMode, str] = {
45	    "executive": (
46	        "Target: P1 (B2B Executive Leader) and P2 (PE/Board/VC). "
47	        "Altitude: strategic. Opens with business problem, not technical solution. "
48	        "States the so-what explicitly at every section. "
49	        "No unexplained acronyms or jargon. "
50	        "Narrative arc: problem → current state gap → what we build → what changes. "
51	        "BCG/McKinsey presentation standard: clean hierarchy, bold claims supported "
52	        "immediately, no decorative content. Rewards 90-second skimming."
53	    ),
54	    "practitioner": (
55	        "Target: P3 (CDO/CAIO/CDAIO/CISO). "
56	        "Altitude: methodological + operational. "
57	        "Opens with credibility signals: pre-registration, gate structure, null results. "
58	        "Peer-level depth — no over-explaining. "
59	        "Operational authenticity: describes real complexity, does not sanitize. "
60	        "References tools by name. Specificity is respect."
61	    ),
62	    "technical": (
63	        "Target: Developer/contributor. "
64	        "Altitude: implementation. "
65	        "Architecture, interfaces, dependencies, test coverage, how to run, how to extend. "
66	        "Dense but navigable. Code blocks and precise naming."
67	    ),
68	}
69	
70	
71	@dataclass
72	class DocumentationRequest:
73	    """
74	    A request to generate or update a documentation artifact.
75	
76	    All fields required. Mode determines altitude, voice, and format.
77	    Prior document content is read before any rewrite — no blind overwrites.
78	    """
79	    document_path: str              # relative path in repo, e.g. "OVERVIEW.md"
80	    mode: DocumentationMode         # executive | practitioner | technical
81	    trigger: str                    # what prompted this update, e.g. "Phase 0 complete"
82	    prior_content: str = ""         # current file content — populated before generation
83	    research_phase: str = "pre-execution"  # current platform phase
84	    compliance_required: bool = True  # executive mode always requires branding compliance
85	
86	
87	@dataclass
88	class DocumentationResult:
89	    """
90	    Output of a documentation generation run.
91	    """
92	    document_path: str
93	    mode: DocumentationMode
94	    content: str
95	    compliance_passed: bool | None = None  # None = not yet reviewed
96	    compliance_issues: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
97	    run_id: str = ""
98	    notes: str = ""
99	
100	
101	class RepoDocumentationAgent(BaseResearchAgent):
102	    """
103	    Audience-stratified documentation agent for the HC-GRC repository.
104	
105	    NOT a protected research agent — documentation does not affect research
106	    design and may be autonomously optimized by Agent Evolution.
107	
108	    Dispatches by DocumentationMode, not by research phase. Can run in
109	    any phase (exploratory, confirmatory) for non-findings documentation.
110	    For executive summaries of findings: Gate 4 must be confirmed before
111	    findings are described as conclusions.
112	    """
113	
114	    PROTECTED = False
115	    AGENT_ID = "repo-documentation"
116	
117	    def __init__(
118	        self,
119	        repo_root: Path | str = Path("."),
120	        docs_output_dir: Path | str = Path("reports/executive-summary"),
121	    ) -> None:
122	        self.repo_root = Path(repo_root)
123	        self.docs_output_dir = Path(docs_output_dir)
124	
125	    def run_exploratory(self, state: HCGRCState) -> dict[str, Any]:
126	        """
127	        Generate or update documentation artifacts.
128	
129	        Safe to run in exploratory phase for all documentation modes EXCEPT
130	        executive summaries of research findings (which require Gate 4).
131	        """
132	        raise NotImplementedError(
133	            "Documentation generation not yet implemented. "
134	            "Phase 1 implementation will: "
135	            "(1) read DocumentationRequest from state, "
136	            "(2) load mode context from _MODE_CONTEXT, "
137	            "(3) read current document content via mcp-github, "
138	            "(4) generate updated content respecting mode altitude and voice architecture, "
139	            "(5) for executive mode: pass to Branding Compliance Agent before commit, "
140	            "(6) commit via GitHub Management Agent, "
141	            "(7) log to lab notebook."
142	        )
143	
144	    def run_confirmatory(self, state: HCGRCState) -> dict[str, Any]:
145	        """
146	        Generate executive summaries of confirmed research findings.
147	
148	        Requires Gate 4 approval before findings described as conclusions.
149	        Executive summaries of unconfirmed findings are a misrepresentation.
150	        """
151	        raise NotImplementedError(
152	            "Confirmatory documentation (executive summaries of findings) "
153	            "not implemented until findings are available. "
154	            "Requires Gate 4 approval — confirmed results only."
155	        )
156	
157	    def _get_mode_context(self, mode: DocumentationMode) -> str:
158	        """Return the audience altitude context string for the given mode."""
159	        return _MODE_CONTEXT[mode]
160	
161	    def _requires_compliance_review(self, request: DocumentationRequest) -> bool:
162	        """
163	        Determine whether Branding Compliance Agent review is required.
164	
165	        Executive mode always requires review.
166	        Practitioner and technical modes require review when content
167	        includes independence claims or commercial disclosures.
168	        """
169	        return request.mode == "executive" or request.compliance_required
170	
171	    def _assert_findings_gated(self, state: HCGRCState, mode: DocumentationMode) -> None:
172	        """
173	        For executive mode documents describing research findings:
174	        assert Gate 4 is approved before framing findings as conclusions.
175	
176	        Executive summaries of unconfirmed findings are a misrepresentation.
177	        This check applies only when the document contains finding claims —
178	        pre-execution and phase documentation are exempt.
179	        """
180	        if mode != "executive":
181	            return
182	        gate_status = state.get("gate_status", {})
183	        gate_4 = gate_status.get("gate_4")
184	        if gate_4 is None or gate_4.get("decision") != "approved":
185	            raise SAPViolationError(
186	                "Executive summaries describing research findings require Gate 4 approval. "
187	                "Findings described as conclusions before confirmatory review is complete "
188	                "is a misrepresentation. Current Gate 4 status: "
189	                f"{gate_4.get('decision') if gate_4 else 'not_run'}."
190	            )

13	
14	# Repo Documentation Agent
15	
16	## Purpose
17	
18	Three different people will land on this repository. A PE operating partner doing diligence on a portfolio company's security posture. A CISO evaluating whether the research methodology is rigorous enough to cite. A ML engineer deciding whether to contribute. Each one needs something different — and none of them will read past the first document that fails to speak to them.
19	
20	This agent maintains three parallel documentation layers, each written for one of those readers, so none of them hit a ceiling. Executive documentation reads like a McKinsey brief. Practitioner documentation reads like it was written by someone who has done the job. Technical documentation reads like code.
21	
22	## Audience Taxonomy
23	
24	The three documentation modes map directly to the Hipster CISO audience profiles:
25	
26	| Mode | Primary Persona | Altitude | Voice | Format |
27	|------|----------------|---------|-------|--------|
28	| **executive** | P1 (B2B Executive Leader), P2 (PE/Board/VC) | Business problem + strategic significance | Clear, non-technical, so-what explicit at every level | Narrative prose, minimal tables, no acronyms without definition |
29	| **practitioner** | P3 (CDO/CAIO/CDAIO/CISO) | Methodology + operational implications | Technically credible, operationally authentic, peer-level | Structured, referenced, tools-not-frameworks |
30	| **technical** | Developer/contributor | Implementation detail | Direct, dense, code-forward | README conventions, inline code, precise |
31	
32	### Mode Characteristics
33	
34	**Executive mode (P1/P2):**
35	- Opens with the business problem, not the technical solution
36	- States the "so what" for every section — what this means for decisions, risk, or value
37	- Uses analogies, not jargon. If a term requires technical background, it gets a plain-language bridge
38	- Strategic narrative arc: problem → current state gap → what this builds → what it changes
39	- Visual hierarchy that rewards skimming — a board member reading for 90 seconds should come away with the core thesis
40	- References BCG/McKinsey presentation standards: clean structure, bold claims supported immediately, no decorative content
41	
42	**Practitioner mode (P3):**
43	- Opens with methodological credibility signals — pre-registration, gate structure, null result handling
44	- Operational authenticity: describes the real complexity without sanitizing it
45	- Addresses the career and organizational context: this is research a CDO/CISO could cite, commission, or build on
46	- No over-explaining. Assumes the reader has seen bad GRC research before and knows what to look for
47	- References tools and frameworks by name. Specificity is respect.
48	
49	**Technical mode:**
50	- Implementation-first. Architecture, interfaces, dependencies, test coverage
51	- Contributor-friendly: how to run, how to extend, where things live
52	- Dense but navigable — headers, code blocks, precise naming
53	
54	## Position in Workflow
55	
56	```
57	Platform milestone or new artifact
58	        ↓
59	[Repo Documentation Agent]
60	  ├── Identify which documentation layer(s) are affected
61	  ├── Generate/update content in affected mode(s)
62	  ├── Branding Compliance Agent review
63	  │       ↓ PASS              ↓ FAIL
64	  │   Commit via           Return with
65	  │  GitHub Agent       remediation notes
66	  └── Lab notebook entry
67	```
68	
69	## PR Integration — Drift Prevention
70	
71	Documentation that doesn't track code is eventually fiction. The repo-documentation agent fires on every pull request via `.github/workflows/docs-sync.yml`. It maps every changed file to the documentation layers it affects, identifies drift, and blocks merge if critical documentation is stale.
72	
73	### File → Documentation Impact Map
74	
75	| Changed files | Documentation layers affected |
76	|--------------|-------------------------------|
77	| `agents/**/*.md` | README.md (architecture section), OVERVIEW.md (what-this-builds) |
78	| `src/agents/**` | README.md (architecture, status) |
79	| `configs/**` | README.md (navigating this repository) |
80	| `docs/decisions/**` | README.md (key documents table) |
81	| `docs/protocol/**` | README.md (research design), OVERVIEW.md (research integrity section) |
82	| `src/**/*.py` | README.md (status) |
83	| Gate transition | OVERVIEW.md (where things stand), README.md (status) |
84	| Confirmed findings (Gate 4+) | OVERVIEW.md (findings), reports/executive-summary/ |
85	
86	### PR Workflow
87	
88	```
89	PR opened or updated
90	        ↓
91	[docs-sync.yml] — runs scripts/check_doc_drift.py
92	        ↓
93	Drift detected?
94	   ├── No → PR comment: "Documentation current. No updates required."
95	   └── Yes → PR comment listing affected layers + required changes
96	              If critical drift (confirmed findings without executive summary): block merge
97	```
98	
99	### Documentation Staleness Rules
100	
101	| Drift type | Response |
102	|-----------|---------|
103	| New agent card, no README update | Warning comment — update required before merge |
104	| New config, no navigation update | Warning comment |
105	| Confirmed finding, no OVERVIEW update | Block merge — executive-layer misrepresentation risk |
106	| Phase transition, no status update | Block merge |
107	| Any change, no PR template checklist completed | Block merge |
108	
109	## Document Map
110	
111	| Document | Mode | Location | Audience |
112	|----------|------|----------|---------|
113	| OVERVIEW.md | Executive | repo root | P1, P2 |
114	| README.md | Practitioner | repo root | P3, technical |
115	| docs/charter/PROJECT_CHARTER.md | Technical | docs/ | Developer |
116	| docs/architecture/AGENT_WORKFLOW.md | Technical | docs/ | Developer |
117	| RESEARCH_BRIEF.md | Executive | repo root | P1, P2 — one-page investment thesis |
118	| reports/executive-summary/ | Executive | reports/ | P1, P2 — generated post-findings |
119	
120	## Inputs
121	
122	| Input | Source | Required |
123	|-------|--------|---------|
124	| Research milestone or new finding | Orchestrator / pipeline | Yes |
125	| Target mode | Calling agent or human | Yes — executive, practitioner, or technical |
126	| Brand guidelines | User preferences (Sections 2–3) | Yes |
127	| Audience profile | hipsterciso-audience-profiles skill | Yes |
128	| Prior document version | GitHub (via mcp-github) | Yes — no rewrites without reading current state |
129	
130	## Outputs / Artifacts
131	
132	| Artifact | Destination | Mode | Notes |
133	|----------|-------------|------|-------|
134	| OVERVIEW.md | repo root | Executive | Plain-language entry point for P1/P2 |
135	| README.md | repo root | Practitioner | Methodology-forward, P3-native |
136	| RESEARCH_BRIEF.md | repo root | Executive | One-page thesis — strategic case for the research program. Created at Gate 3; blocked from findings content until Gate 4. |
137	| GitHub repo description | GitHub metadata | Executive | 160 chars, no jargon |
138	| GitHub topics | GitHub metadata | Technical | Discoverability tags |
139	| Compliance log | Lab notebook | — | Branding Compliance Agent review result + human sign-off; log entry with reviewer name and date before any executive-mode commit |
140	
141	### RESEARCH_BRIEF.md Format Specification
142	
143	One page, four sections, no jargon, no unexplained acronyms:
144	
145	1. **The Question** — What empirical question this research answers and why it has not been answered before. Opens with the business problem, not the technical approach.
146	2. **The Three Structural Differentiators** — Independent (no vendor funding or influence), pre-registered (hypotheses committed before data analysis), designed to scale (framework-agnostic platform that compounds across studies).
147	3. **Current State** — Where the program stands today. No findings language before Gate 4. Before Gate 4: describe platform completion and timeline. After Gate 4: describe confirmed findings with confidence intervals.
148	4. **What It Changes** — What board conversations, PE diligence, and practitioner decision-making look different when this research is complete. "So what" is explicit.
149	
150	Creation trigger: Gate 3 completion (EDA review approved). Gate 4 required before any findings language is included.
151	
152	## Tools & MCP Servers
153	
154	| Tool | Purpose | Notes |
155	|------|---------|-------|
156	| mcp-github | Read current docs; update repo description, topics, files | All commits via GitHub Management Agent |
157	| mcp-lab-notebook | Log documentation decisions and compliance outcomes | Append-only |
158	
159	## Skills Used
160	
161	- **Doc Coauthoring** — Structured workflow for transferring context into documentation that works for readers. Applied to all three modes: ensures the document solves the reader's problem, not just describes the platform's structure.
162	
163	## Handoffs
164	
165	**Receives from**: Orchestrator (phase transitions), any agent producing findings with documentation implications
166	**Passes to**: Branding Compliance Agent (executive-mode content before commit), GitHub Management Agent (commit after compliance review)
167	**Human gate**: Executive-mode documents require Branding Compliance Agent review, then human sign-off before commit. Human reviewer confirms voice compliance, independence claim accuracy, and Gate 4 status; logs entry in lab notebook with reviewer name and date.
168	
169	## Behavioral Constraints
170	
171	- Executive-mode content must pass Branding Compliance Agent review before commit. The agent checks voice compliance, independence claim accuracy, and Gate 4 status. Human reviewer signs off with lab notebook entry (reviewer name and date).
172	- Never conflate documentation modes — executive content must not contain unexplained technical jargon; practitioner content must not be dumbed down to executive altitude
173	- Always read the current version of a document before rewriting it — no blind overwrites. On rewrite, verify that all required sections (see DRIFT_MAP REQUIRED_SECTIONS) are present in the output.
174	- Executive documents use the voice architecture from user preferences Section 3 — premium executive rigor, honest contrarianism, warm authority. Not sanitized corporate voice.
175	- Never publish research findings in executive framing before the findings have cleared Gate 4 (confirmatory results review) — executive summaries of unconfirmed findings are a misrepresentation
176	- Never include SCF data or SCF-derived content (verbatim, paraphrase, or structural derivative) in any public-facing document — CC BY-ND 4.0 prohibits redistribution of derivative works
177	- Independence claims in executive content must be grounded against current vendor relationships — any vendor dependency that qualifies the independence claim must be disclosed or the claim must be qualified
178	- GitHub repo description and topics are updated at every phase transition — stale metadata is misleading
179	
180	## Failure Modes & Recovery
181	
182	| Failure | Detection | Recovery |
183	|---------|-----------|----------|
184	| Human branding review returns issues | Reviewer log entry lists concerns | Apply specific remediation; do not resubmit with same content |
185	| Executive document uses unexplained jargon | Self-review pass against mode checklist | Rewrite flagged terms with plain-language bridges |
186	| Document conflicts with confirmed research findings | Accuracy check in human review | Correct document; log discrepancy in lab notebook |
187	| SCF-derived content detected in public document | Human review or PR security checklist | Remove entirely — no paraphrase is safe under CC BY-ND |
188	| Independence claim unsubstantiated | Human review against vendor register | Qualify the claim or disclose the dependency |
189	| GitHub API unavailable | mcp-github error | Draft locally; queue for commit when restored |
190	
191	## Evaluation Criteria
192	
193	- [ ] All three documentation modes current within one sprint of any phase transition
194	- [ ] Executive documents reviewed by Branding Compliance Agent before every commit
195	- [ ] GitHub repo description and topics reflect current platform scope
196	- [ ] OVERVIEW.md readable by a board member with no technical background
197	- [ ] README.md contains sufficient methodological detail for a CDO/CISO to evaluate rigor
198	- [ ] No executive-mode document references unconfirmed findings as conclusions
199

9	## 1. Purpose and Positioning
10	
11	The hc-grc program is a modular, scientifically rigorous research platform for the empirical characterization of cybersecurity and data privacy control space. It is designed to grow beyond any single research question into a durable analytical infrastructure — the equivalent of a software product, not a one-off analysis.
12	
13	The primary corpus is the Secure Controls Framework (SCF), used as both a research subject and an alignment substrate. The SCF's 1,400+ controls, 200+ STRM-mapped Laws/Regulations/Frameworks (LRFs), 39 risk categories, 6-level maturity model, and risk/threat crosswalks constitute a uniquely rich, openly licensed, machine-readable dataset for control space analysis.
14	
15	**The core tension the platform exploits**: The SCF claims Expert-Derived Content (EDC) produces superior crosswalk mappings versus NLP/ML approaches. No empirical validation of those 280,000+ mappings has ever been published. Every analytical module in this platform addresses some dimension of that gap — directly or indirectly.
16	
17	**Strategic output**: Published research, practitioner-facing tools, and commercially deployable analytical products that serve the three audiences defined in the project's user personas.
18

21	## 1.5 Strategic Program Context
22	
23	**This project is Tier 1 of a three-tier research program.** Every design decision — data schemas, ARA artifact formats, graph data models, null result handling — is made with Tier 2 and Tier 3 as downstream consumers.
24	
25	### The Three Tiers
26	
27	**Tier 1 — Framework Science (this project, plus future framework projects)**
28	
29	The SCF is simultaneously the research subject and the measurement instrument. Its ~280,000 STRM mappings are structured, testable claims: "SCF control X has relationship type R to framework Y control Z." The platform does not accept those claims — it tests them. Per-framework knowledge graphs are the primary output. Null results — mappings that fail empirical testing — are first-class findings, not failures.
30	
31	Future Tier 1 projects: `hc-grc-nist-800-53`, `hc-grc-nist-800-82`, `hc-grc-cis-v8`. Each follows identical methodology (P1–P5 modules, same LangGraph architecture, same gate structure). Each is an independent project to preserve pre-registration integrity — no framework project's confirmatory data contaminates another's hypothesis formation.
32	
33	**Tier 2 — Comparative Analysis (future: `hc-grc-comparative`)**
34	
35	Ingests all Tier 1 knowledge graphs. Answers cross-framework questions: where does the GRC landscape genuinely converge? Which controls are empirically load-bearing across multiple independent frameworks? What is the blast radius — if control X fails, what cascades through the cross-framework topology? What are the underlying control archetypes the industry has reproduced under dozens of different names?
36	
37	This tier requires two or more Tier 1 projects completed before it starts.
38	
39	**Tier 3 — Organizational Impact Modeling (deferred: `hc-grc-impact`)**
40	
41	The commercially significant tier. Takes Tier 2's empirical findings and models them against real organizations to quantify: which control failures drive financial losses, and by how much? Not correlation — causal inference (causal forests, do-calculus) so the answer is actionable. SHAP values provide the explainability layer. Monte Carlo simulation produces probability distributions over financial outcomes with confidence intervals.
42	
43	Tier 3 is deferred. It requires Tier 2 outputs and external data (breach databases, financial loss records) with privacy and consent constraints outside the current scope. It is documented here because Tier 1 design decisions must not foreclose Tier 3 feasibility.
44	
45	### Design Requirements Flowing from This Context
46	
47	1. **ARA artifact schema must be framework-agnostic.** The knowledge graph export must work identically for NIST 800-53, CIS v8, and SCF. No SCF-specific assumptions in the schema.
48	2. **Null results are structured identically to positive findings.** Tier 2 and Tier 3 consume the full result distribution; suppressed or unstructured null results break the pipeline.
49	3. **Confidence scores on all findings.** Tier 3 uses these as ML model features. If findings don't carry confidence scores, the Tier 3 feature space is impoverished.
50	4. **P2 topology graphs must be designed for multi-graph composition.** The blast radius model in Tier 2 runs graph diffusion across the combined cross-framework topology. P2's data model must support it.
51	
52	See ADR-0010 for the full strategic program decision record.

56	## 2. Scope
57	
58	All five analytical modules (P1–P5) are in scope. They are not sequential — they are parallel research tracks that share infrastructure, data models, and findings. Module outputs feed each other.
59	
60	| Module | Name | Primary Question |
61	|--------|------|-----------------|
62	| P1 | STRM Calibration | Do the SCF's expert STRM mappings hold up under NLP-based semantic analysis? |
63	| P2 | Control Space Topology | Do the 33 editorial domains correspond to natural semantic clusters? |
64	| P3 | Regulatory Convergence | Which control domains have multi-jurisdictional consensus, and which are outliers? |
65	| P4 | Risk Blindspot Engine | Which of the 39 risk categories are systematically underserved by standard framework portfolios? |
66	| P5 | AI Governance Cluster Analysis | Which controls across all 33 domains are semantically entangled with AI governance — not just AAT? |
67	
68	P5 is explicitly **not** an AAT domain filter. It is a cross-domain clustering problem: let the ML identify which controls across the entire SCF are semantically relevant to AI governance when the data, not the editorial taxonomy, makes that determination.

191	## 5. Module Research Designs
192	
193	### P1 — STRM Calibration: Auditing the Auditors
194	
195	**Research question**: Do the SCF's expert-derived STRM mappings (relationship type and strength score) correspond to empirically measurable semantic relationships between control text and LRF requirement text?
196	
197	**Why it matters**: The SCF's commercial and IP positioning rests on the claim that EDC produces superior mappings. This is the first empirical test of that claim at scale.
198	
199	#### Hypotheses
200	
201	| ID | Hypothesis | Null | Test |
202	|----|-----------|------|------|
203	| H1.1 | STRM strength scores (1–10) are positively correlated with embedding cosine similarity | ρ = 0 | Spearman rank correlation + permutation test |
204	| H1.2 | STRM relationship type is predictable from embedding similarity distributions | Accuracy = random baseline | Multi-class classification (logistic regression, SVM, fine-tuned BERT) |
205	| H1.3 | "Equal To" (=) mappings cluster at higher cosine similarity than "Intersects" (∩) | No distributional difference across types | Kruskal-Wallis H-test |
206	| H1.4 | STRM strength score calibration varies systematically by SCF domain | Calibration is uniform across domains | ANOVA / Kruskal-Wallis by domain group |
207	| H1.5 | NIST-cluster frameworks (800-53, CSF, 800-171) show higher NLP-STRM agreement than cross-cluster pairs | No difference | Mann-Whitney U test |
208	| H1.6 | High-strength STRM mappings (score ≥ 8) exhibit significantly higher NLP similarity than low-strength mappings (score ≤ 3) | No difference | Mann-Whitney U test |
209	
210	#### Algorithm Inventory (exhaustive — all to be evaluated)
211	
212	**Text Representation**
213	- Sparse: TF-IDF (unigram, bigram, trigram), BM25/Okapi BM25, Boolean n-gram
214	- Dense word: Word2Vec CBOW, Word2Vec Skip-gram, GloVe (6B, 42B), fastText (character n-grams)
215	- Dense sentence: sentence-BERT (all-mpnet-base-v2, all-MiniLM-L6-v2, paraphrase-multilingual-mpnet-base-v2), Universal Sentence Encoder (v4), InferSent (v2)
216	- Contextual: BERT (bert-base-uncased, bert-large-uncased), RoBERTa-base, RoBERTa-large, DeBERTa-v3-base, DeBERTa-v3-large
217	- Domain-specific: SecBERT, CySecBERT, SecurityBERT (cybersecurity domain pre-training)
218	- Instruction-tuned: E5-large-instruct, GTE-large-en-v1.5, BGE-large-en-v1.5, Nomic-embed-text-v1.5
219	
220	**Similarity / Distance Measures**

222	- Euclidean (L2) distance
223	- Manhattan (L1) distance
224	- Dot product
225	- Jaccard similarity (token overlap, bigram overlap)
226	- Word Mover's Distance (WMD) via Gensim
227	- SoftWMD (relaxed WMD)

233	
234	**Classification (predict STRM relationship type)**

238	- Random Forest (100, 500 trees)
239	- Gradient Boosting (XGBoost, LightGBM, CatBoost)

285	### P2 — Control Space Topology
286	
287	**Research question**: Do the SCF's 33 editorial domains correspond to natural semantic communities in the control graph, or are they organizational artifacts that obscure the actual structure of control space?
288	
289	#### Hypotheses
290	
291	| ID | Hypothesis | Null | Test |
292	|----|-----------|------|------|
293	| H2.1 | Community detection on the STRM-weighted control graph produces communities that differ significantly from the 33 editorial domains | Editorial partition = optimal community partition | Compare modularity Q of editorial partition vs. discovered partition (NMI, ARI) |
294	| H2.2 | Some SCF domains are structurally isolated (low cross-domain connectivity) while others are structurally central (high cross-domain connectivity) | Degree centrality is uniform across domain nodes | Degree centrality distribution test; identify hubs and isolates |
295	| H2.3 | The 39 risk categories form natural clusters in the control graph that cut across editorial domain lines | Risk categories cluster within, not across, domains | Community detection seeded by risk category labels; modularity comparison |
296	| H2.4 | STRM relationship type (⊂, ∩, =, ⊃) predicts edge weight in the control graph independently of strength score | Relationship type adds no information beyond strength score | Partial correlation; regression with both predictors |
297	| H2.5 | Graph density varies systematically across the 33 domains | Density is uniform | Chi-square on degree distribution by domain |
298	
299	**Algorithm Inventory**
300	- Graph construction: NetworkX, PyG, iGraph
301	- Community detection: Louvain, Leiden, Girvan-Newman, Label Propagation, Infomap, Spectral Clustering, METIS, Markov Clustering (MCL)
302	- Centrality measures: Degree, Betweenness, Closeness, Eigenvector, PageRank, HITS
303	- Graph embeddings: Node2Vec, DeepWalk, GraphSAGE, GCN, GAT
304	- Comparison metrics: Normalized Mutual Information (NMI), Adjusted Rand Index (ARI), Variation of Information, Modularity Q
305	- Visualization: UMAP, t-SNE, ForceAtlas2 (Gephi-compatible), Kamada-Kawai
306

309	### P3 — Regulatory Convergence Atlas
310	
311	**Research question**: Which SCF control domains have multi-jurisdictional regulatory consensus, and which are dominated by single-jurisdiction mandates?
312	
313	#### Hypotheses
314	
315	| ID | Hypothesis | Null | Test |
316	|----|-----------|------|------|
317	| H3.1 | LRFs cluster into groups that share control domain emphasis patterns, independent of geographic region | Clustering by control domain profile matches geographic clustering | Compare cluster assignments: domain-profile-based vs. geography-based (ARI) |
318	| H3.2 | A small subset of SCF domains (< 20%) accounts for > 60% of all STRM mappings | STRM mappings are uniformly distributed across domains | Pareto analysis; chi-square goodness-of-fit test |
319	| H3.3 | STRM relationship type distribution (⊂ vs. ∩ vs. = vs. ⊃) varies significantly by LRF category (law vs. regulation vs. framework) | Distribution is uniform across categories | Chi-square test on contingency table |
320	| H3.4 | Regulatory convergence (number of independent LRFs mapping to a domain) predicts SCF control density in that domain | No correlation | Spearman correlation: mapping count vs. control count by domain |
321	
322	**Algorithm Inventory**
323	- TF-IDF-style domain coverage vectors (LRF × domain matrix, weighted by STRM type and strength)
324	- Hierarchical agglomerative clustering (Ward, complete, average, single linkage)
325	- K-means (k selected by silhouette coefficient and elbow method)
326	- DBSCAN / HDBSCAN
327	- Non-negative Matrix Factorization (NMF) for latent regulatory theme discovery
328	- LDA topic modeling on LRF requirement text
329	- Cosine similarity matrix → heatmap
330	- t-SNE / UMAP for LRF positioning in domain-coverage space
331	- Gephi / NetworkX for LRF-domain bipartite graph visualization

335	### P4 — Risk Blindspot Engine
336	
337	**Research question**: Given a specific portfolio of compliance frameworks, which of the 39 SCF risk categories remain systematically underserved — and what is the materiality exposure implied by those gaps?
338	
339	#### Hypotheses
340	
341	| ID | Hypothesis | Null | Test |
342	|----|-----------|------|------|
343	| H4.1 | Framework portfolio coverage of the 39 risk categories is non-uniform — some risks are well-covered by nearly all portfolios, others are chronically underserved | Coverage is uniform across 39 risk categories | Chi-square test on coverage distribution |
344	| H4.2 | Common practitioner-facing framework combinations (e.g., NIST CSF + SOC 2; HIPAA + NIST 800-171) leave at least one risk group (R-SC or R-GV) with < 40% coverage | Coverage ≥ 40% across all groups for standard portfolios | Coverage scoring per portfolio; threshold analysis |
345	| H4.3 | MCR controls provide better risk coverage than DSR controls within the same domain | No difference | Mann-Whitney U on risk coverage scores: MCR vs. DSR by domain |
346	| H4.4 | Risk coverage gaps predicted by framework portfolio composition can be identified by a classifier with > 75% accuracy | Accuracy = baseline (majority class) | Multi-label classification: portfolio features → gap labels |
347	
348	**Algorithm Inventory**
349	- Coverage matrix construction: binary (control present/absent) and weighted (STRM strength × relationship type)
350	- Set cover analysis: greedy approximation of minimum LRF set for maximum risk coverage
351	- Integer Linear Programming for exact minimum set cover (small instances)
352	- Multi-label classification (portfolio → gap prediction): Binary Relevance, Label Powerset, Classifier Chains
353	- Materiality scoring: tie risk exposure scores to SCF financial materiality thresholds (≥5% pre-tax income proxy)
354	- Pareto frontier analysis: coverage gain per additional framework
355	- Shapley values for framework contribution attribution
356

359	### P5 — AI Governance Cluster Analysis
360	
361	**Research question**: Which controls across all 33 SCF domains are semantically entangled with AI governance — as determined by NLP clustering, not editorial domain assignment?
362	
363	This module explicitly rejects the AAT domain filter as its starting point. The hypothesis is that AI governance implicates controls across many domains (IAC, DCH, TPM, GOV, RSK, MON, etc.) and that the AAT editorial boundary understates the true cross-domain footprint of AI governance requirements.
364	
365	#### Hypotheses
366	
367	| ID | Hypothesis | Null | Test |
368	|----|-----------|------|------|
369	| H5.1 | A significant portion (> 25%) of AI governance-relevant SCF controls fall outside the AAT domain when identified via NLP clustering rather than editorial assignment | AI governance controls are confined to AAT | Cross-domain percentage of AI governance cluster; chi-square vs. expected AAT concentration |
370	| H5.2 | AI governance LRFs (EU AI Act, NIST AI RMF) map to SCF controls in a cross-domain pattern that the AAT domain alone cannot satisfy | AI governance LRF mappings are satisfied by AAT controls alone | Coverage analysis: AI LRFs vs. AAT-only controls vs. full AI governance cluster |
371	| H5.3 | AI governance controls form identifiable sub-clusters by risk type (data integrity, model governance, transparency, accountability) that cut across the 33 editorial domains | AI governance controls form one undifferentiated cluster | Sub-cluster analysis; label by AI risk taxonomy |
372	| H5.4 | The cross-domain AI governance cluster can be identified with > 80% precision from embedding space alone (without using domain labels as features) | Domain-label-free classification does not achieve > 80% precision | Binary classification: AI governance relevance predicted from embeddings |
373	
374	**Algorithm Inventory**
375	- Embedding all 1,400+ controls (same models as P1)
376	- Embedding AI governance LRF requirement texts (EU AI Act, NIST AI RMF, SCF DPMP, ISO/IEC 42001 if available)
377	- Semantic similarity scoring: control ↔ AI governance requirement space
378	- Thresholding + binary classification to label "AI governance relevant" controls across all 33 domains
379	- K-means, HDBSCAN, BERTopic clustering on the labeled set
380	- Sub-cluster labeling via NLI-based topic assignment
381	- Cross-domain visualization: UMAP with domain color, AI governance overlay
382	- Comparison: SCF AAT domain controls vs. NLP-identified AI governance cluster (NMI, ARI, set overlap)
383	- AI governance coverage gap: which AI LRF requirements are unaddressed by AAT alone but satisfied by the full cluster?

410	## 7. Cross-Cutting Requirements
411	
412	### Reproducibility
413	- All random seeds fixed and documented per experiment
414	- Data versioning: source files archived with SHA256 hash + download date
415	- Embedding cache: stored with model name + version in filename
416	- MLflow: every experiment logged with parameters, metrics, artifacts
417	
418	### Scientific Rigor
419	- Every module's hypotheses pre-registered before analysis begins (documented in research/ directory)
420	- Multiple comparisons corrected (Bonferroni and/or Benjamini-Hochberg)
421	- Effect sizes reported alongside p-values
422	- Null results documented and published — a "no significant difference" finding is a finding
423	- Confidence intervals on all point estimates (bootstrap where parametric assumptions don't hold)
424	
425	### Modularity Contract
426	- Every `src/` module is independently importable with no circular dependencies
427	- Every module accepts typed inputs from the canonical schema and returns typed outputs
428	- Analysis modules may depend on `src/` library modules but not on each other's outputs directly — outputs are written to `data/03-processed/` or `experiments/` and consumed by downstream modules via explicit file reads
429	
430	### Extensibility Gates
431	- Adding a new framework: add a parser in `src/ingestion/`, add a `Framework` record, add a data acquisition note in this charter, ingest to `data/01-raw/`
432	- Adding a new embedding model: add to the embedding engine registry in `src/embeddings/`; all downstream modules consume from the embedding cache, so no changes elsewhere
433	- Adding a new P-module: add a research design file in `research/`, add an experiment directory, add `src/` components as needed

441	**Critical IP boundary**: The STRM calibration findings in P1 are themselves original analytical work — the comparison between NLP-derived similarity scores and SCF expert scores is not a derivative of the SCF. The methodology and findings are independently copyrightable research outputs.
442	
443	The SCF's explicit argument that EDC > NLP for IP reasons does not preclude analyzing those mappings empirically. Observation ≠ reproduction.
444	
445	---
446	
447	## 8.5 Program Governance — Tier Completion and Escalation
448	
449	*Established via issues #91 and #94. Supersedes the original auto-escalation language in Section 1.5.*
450	
451	### Execution Model
452	
453	The platform is an autonomous research organism (ADR-0012). Thomas builds and governs the infrastructure; the platform conducts the research. Thomas approves Charter amendments and tier completion; the platform handles all execution within approved scope.
454	
455	### Tier 1 Completion Criteria
456	
457	Tier 1 is complete when **both** of the following are true:
458	
459	1. **SCF data space fully characterized** — platform self-assessed. The platform evaluates diminishing returns across active research threads. When no active module is generating findings of sufficient novelty or business significance, the platform generates a completion assessment with supporting evidence.
460	
461	2. **At least one Tier 1 finding published** — preprint (arXiv) counts. The publication serves two purposes simultaneously: community influence (primary strategic objective) and context base for Tier 2 exploration. The publication informs Tier 2 possibilities; it does not pre-specify Tier 2 hypotheses.
462	
463	Tier 1 completion is **platform-proposed, Thomas-approved**: the platform generates a Tier 1 completion report, Thomas reviews and approves. Tier 1 is not done until the completion report is approved.
464	
465	### Tier Escalation Model
466	
467	**T1 → T2 is a completion gate, not a quality gate.**
468	
469	Tier 2 executes when Tier 1 is complete — unconditionally. The quality of Tier 1 findings does not gate Tier 2. Cross-framework comparison is worth conducting regardless of whether Tier 1 findings are strong or null. Null results in Tier 1 are findings; they do not block Tier 2.
470	
471	If the Tier 1 platform generates cross-framework intelligence (questions comparing SCF to other frameworks), that is a **Charter violation** — the scope boundary is broken, not the findings. This triggers Escalation routing per ADR-0012: park the finding, notify Thomas, correct scope. Tier 2 does not begin early because interesting cross-framework questions emerge.
472	
473	**T2 → T3 is a prerequisites gate.**
474	
475	Tier 3 has genuine blocking prerequisites that do not currently exist:
476	- External breach/loss data licensing
477	- Privacy and consent framework design
478	- Ethics review
479	
480	Tier 3 cannot proceed until all three prerequisites are independently satisfied. The platform monitors T3 prerequisites passively but cannot self-authorize T3 entry under any conditions. Thomas initiates T3 feasibility work explicitly.
481	
482	### Tier 2 and Tier 3 Completion
483	
484	Follow the same two-condition model as Tier 1, adapted to their scope:
485	- Fully characterized data space for that tier's domain
486	- At least one published finding from that tier
487	
488	### Single-Operator Independence Model
489	
490	This is a single-operator program. Thomas Jones is the researcher, operator, and Charter approver. There is no independent co-reviewer.
491	
492	Independence from researcher bias is achieved through structural transparency, not co-reviewer availability. This was an explicit design decision (see issue #89) — evaluated alternatives included OSF pre-registration and an independent Gate 2 co-reviewer; both were declined as incompatible with the autonomous iterative model.
493	
494	The independence mechanisms are:
495	
496	- **Code gates** — Gate 2 is deterministic code that cannot be bypassed. The platform cannot enter confirmatory analysis without an approval event being cryptographically logged.
497	- **Append-only provenance** — the W3C PROV-DM audit trail records every platform action before the next step executes. No step can be retroactively removed.
498	- **Timestamped pre-registration** — every hypothesis the platform pursues is logged to the Preregistration Ledger with an RFC 3161 timestamp before confirmatory testing begins.
499	- **Complete reproducibility package** — all data, code, configurations, and logs are published with findings so any reviewer can reconstruct the full decision history.
500	
501	This is documented as a deliberate methodological position, not an oversight. The manuscript will state it explicitly.
502	
503	---
504	
505	## 9. Open Questions (To Be Resolved Before Build Begins)
506	
507	1. **Historical version analysis**: SCF releases quarterly. How many historical versions should the initial corpus include? Version-comparison analysis (how the control graph evolves over time) is a potential P6 module.
508	2. **Paywalled frameworks**: ISO 27001, PCI DSS full text, HITRUST — at what point do we pursue licensing? These would significantly expand the LRF corpus in P3 and P4.
509	3. **Deliverable format**: Is the primary output (a) published research, (b) a practitioner-facing tool, (c) a commercial analytical product, or (d) all three in sequence? This affects how the reporting layer is built.
510	4. **Compute environment**: Embedding 1,400+ controls × 200+ LRF requirement sets at scale will require GPU time. Local (M-series Mac) may suffice for prototyping; cloud (Lambda Labs, Vast.ai) for production runs.
511	5. **STRM PDFs vs. Excel**: The SCF sells STRM Excel bundles ($25) — the free download includes STRM in the main Excel. Clarify whether the main download has all STRM data or only a subset, before designing the ingestion pipeline.
512

1	"""
2	HC-GRC agent base types and contracts.
3	
4	All Phase 1 research agents inherit from BaseResearchAgent or implement the
5	same __call__ signature. The contract is:
6	
7	    agent(state: HCGRCState) -> dict[str, Any]
8	
9	The return dict is a LangGraph partial state update — merged into HCGRCState
10	by the graph runtime. Agents must never mutate state in place.
11	
12	PROTECTED agents (P1–P5, statistical-analyst, hypothesis-formalizer) have
13	PROTECTED = True. Agent Evolution may not modify their prompts autonomously.
14	Any modification requires Escalation approval (ADR-0015 §77).
15	
16	Phase guard:
17	  - Exploratory phase: access train_ids + val_ids only
18	  - Confirmatory phase: access test_ids — only after Gate 2 approval
19	  - Agents check state["gate_status"]["gate_2"].decision == "approved" before
20	    touching test split. Violation is a SAP violation.
21	
22	EXP_ prefix convention:
23	  - All exploratory artifacts use EXP_ filename prefix
24	  - No p-value decision language in EXP_ outputs
25	  - Confirmatory scripts carry a SAP header with hypothesis ID and decision rule
26	"""
27	
28	from __future__ import annotations
29	
30	from abc import ABC, abstractmethod
31	from typing import Any, Literal
32	
33	from ..state import HCGRCState
34	
35	# ── Shared input types ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
36	
37	class STRMMapping:
38	    """
39	    A single STRM mapping between two SCF controls.
40	
41	    In Phase 0 (synthetic): populated with placeholder values.
42	    In Phase 1+: populated from data/splits/*.parquet.
43	    """
44	    __slots__ = (
45	        "source_control_id", "target_control_id",
46	        "relationship_type", "strength_score",
47	        "source_domain", "target_domain",
48	        "source_framework", "target_framework",
49	    )
50	
51	    def __init__(
52	        self,
53	        source_control_id: str,
54	        target_control_id: str,
55	        relationship_type: Literal["subset", "superset", "intersection", "equal", "no_relation"],
56	        strength_score: float | None = None,
57	        source_domain: str | None = None,
58	        target_domain: str | None = None,
59	        source_framework: str | None = None,
60	        target_framework: str | None = None,
61	    ) -> None:
62	        self.source_control_id = source_control_id
63	        self.target_control_id = target_control_id
64	        self.relationship_type = relationship_type
65	        self.strength_score = strength_score
66	        self.source_domain = source_domain
67	        self.target_domain = target_domain
68	        self.source_framework = source_framework
69	        self.target_framework = target_framework
70	
71	
72	class ControlMetadata:
73	    """Metadata for a single SCF control node."""
74	    __slots__ = (
75	        "control_id", "domain", "framework",
76	        "maturity_level", "mcr_dsr",
77	    )
78	
79	    def __init__(
80	        self,
81	        control_id: str,
82	        domain: str,
83	        framework: str,
84	        maturity_level: str | None = None,
85	        mcr_dsr: Literal["MCR", "DSR", None] = None,
86	    ) -> None:
87	        self.control_id = control_id
88	        self.domain = domain
89	        self.framework = framework
90	        self.maturity_level = maturity_level
91	        self.mcr_dsr = mcr_dsr
92	
93	
94	# ── Phase guard ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
95	
96	class SAPViolationError(Exception):
97	    """Raised when an agent attempts to access test data before Gate 2."""
98	    pass
99	
100	
101	def assert_gate2_approved(state: HCGRCState) -> None:
102	    """
103	    Raise SAPViolationError if Gate 2 has not been approved.
104	
105	    Must be called before any agent accesses test_ids or test split data.
106	    This is the primary enforcement mechanism for the pre-registration firewall.
107	    """
108	    gate_status = state.get("gate_status", {})
109	    gate_2 = gate_status.get("gate_2")
110	    if gate_2 is None or gate_2.get("decision") != "approved":
111	        raise SAPViolationError(
112	            "Attempted to access test split before Gate 2 approval. "
113	            "This is a SAP violation. Gate 2 status: "
114	            f"{gate_2.get('decision') if gate_2 else 'not_run'}. "
115	            "Do not access test_ids or confirmatory split until gate_2.decision == 'approved'."
116	        )
117	
118	
119	# ── Base agent ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
120	
121	class BaseResearchAgent(ABC):
122	    """
123	    Abstract base for all HC-GRC Phase 1 research agents.
124	
125	    Subclasses implement run_exploratory() and run_confirmatory() separately.
126	    The __call__ method dispatches based on state["phase"].
127	
128	    Protected agents set PROTECTED = True — checked by Agent Evolution before
129	    any prompt modification.
130	    """
131	
132	    PROTECTED: bool = False  # override in P1–P5, statistical-analyst, hypothesis-formalizer
133	    AGENT_ID: str = ""       # kebab-case, matches AGENT.md name field
134	    # Set True once the agent's run_* methods are real. While False, a
135	    # NotImplementedError is an expected Phase-0/1 stub; once True, a
136	    # NotImplementedError is a genuine bug the orchestrator must not silently
137	    # swallow as 'stub_pending' (#202).
138	    IMPLEMENTED: bool = False
139	
140	    def __call__(self, state: HCGRCState) -> dict[str, Any]:
141	        """
142	        LangGraph-compatible call: returns a partial state update dict.
143	
144	        Dispatches to run_exploratory() or run_confirmatory() based on phase.
145	
146	        The canonical phase values written by the graph are phase_0 / phase_1 /
147	        phase_2 (see state.Phase). Exploratory analysis runs in phase_0/phase_1;
148	        confirmatory analysis runs in phase_2, where the Gate 2 SAP firewall
149	        (assert_gate2_approved) must hold before any test-split access. The legacy
150	        string aliases "exploratory"/"confirmatory" are accepted defensively.
151	        (Hardening pass 1, #17: phase_2 previously hit the else-branch and raised,
152	        so run_confirmatory and the SAP firewall were unreachable.)
153	        """
154	        phase = state.get("phase", "phase_0")
155	        if phase in ("phase_0", "phase_1", "exploratory"):
156	            return self.run_exploratory(state)
157	        elif phase in ("phase_2", "confirmatory"):
158	            assert_gate2_approved(state)
159	            return self.run_confirmatory(state)
160	        else:
161	            raise ValueError(f"Unknown phase: {phase!r}")
162	
163	    @abstractmethod
164	    def run_exploratory(self, state: HCGRCState) -> dict[str, Any]:
165	        """
166	        Execute exploratory analysis on train + val splits.
167	
168	        All output artifact filenames must be prefixed EXP_.
169	        No p-value decision language in any exploratory output.
170	        Must not touch test_ids.
171	        """
172	        ...
173	
174	    @abstractmethod
175	    def run_confirmatory(self, state: HCGRCState) -> dict[str, Any]:
176	        """
177	        Execute confirmatory analysis on test split for one hypothesis.
178	
179	        Gate 2 approval is verified by __call__ before this is invoked.
180	        Every confirmatory script must carry a SAP header:
181	          # SAP header: hypothesis_id=H[x].[n], test=[named test],
182	          #             effect_size_threshold=[value], decision_rule=[criterion]
183	        """
184	        ...
185	
186	
187	# ── Base pipeline agent ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
188	
189	class BasePipelineAgent(ABC):
190	    """
191	    Abstract base for data pipeline agents (acquisition, curation, steward, embedding).
192	
193	    Pipeline agents prepare and version the research corpus. They do not perform
194	    exploratory or confirmatory analysis — they have no run_exploratory() /
195	    run_confirmatory() distinction. They run once at project initialization and
196	    re-run when the SCF corpus is updated.
197	
198	    Pipeline agents are never PROTECTED — they contain no analysis logic that
199	    could affect research conclusions. Agent Evolution may modify them without
200	    Escalation approval.
201	
202	    The run() return dict is a partial HCGRCState update, merged by the graph runtime.
203	    Agents must never mutate state in place.
204	    """
205	
206	    AGENT_ID: str = ""     # kebab-case, matches AGENT.md name field
207	    PROTECTED: bool = False  # never True for pipeline agents
208	    IMPLEMENTED: bool = False  # see BaseResearchAgent.IMPLEMENTED (#202)
209	
210	    @abstractmethod
211	    def run(self, state: HCGRCState) -> dict[str, Any]:
212	        """
213	        Execute the pipeline stage and return a partial HCGRCState update.
214	
215	        Raises NotImplementedError until the real SCF data pipeline is live
216	        on the Mac mini with DVC and Qdrant configured.
217	        """
218	        ...

1	"""
2	Gate nodes for the HC-GRC research platform.
3	
4	Five gates enforce human approval at structural transition points.
5	Each gate issues a LangGraph interrupt(), surfacing a structured proposal
6	to the operator. The operator responds via Claude on any device (ADR-0014).
7	
8	Gate decisions are written by gate_coordinator_node — never directly here.
9	On rejection, each gate appends a failure_event for Agent Evolution monitoring.
10	
11	Gate definitions:
12	  Gate 1 — Pre-data checkpoint (Phase 0 complete, all prerequisites verified)
13	  Gate 2 — Exploratory → Confirmatory (hypothesis lock, test data unlock)
14	  Gate 3 — Finding significance review (before publication pipeline)
15	  Gate 4 — Manuscript submission approval
16	  Gate 5 — Escalation resolution gate (triggered by ADR-0014 Escalation loop)
17	"""
18	
19	from __future__ import annotations
20	
21	import datetime
22	from typing import Any
23	
24	from langgraph.types import interrupt
25	
26	from ..state import HCGRCState
27	
28	# ── Shared helpers ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
29	
30	
31	def _build_gate_proposal(gate_id: str, title: str, summary: str,
32	                          checklist: list[str], run_id: str) -> dict[str, Any]:
33	    """Build the structured proposal surfaced to the operator via interrupt()."""
34	    return {
35	        "gate_id": gate_id,
36	        "title": title,
37	        "summary": summary,
38	        "checklist": checklist,
39	        "run_id": run_id,
40	        "timestamp_utc": datetime.datetime.now(datetime.timezone.utc).isoformat(),
41	        "instructions": (
42	            "Respond with: {'decision': 'approved'|'rejected'|'deferred', "
43	            "'rationale': '<your reasoning>'}"
44	        ),
45	    }
46	
47	
48	def _rejection_event(gate_id: str, rationale: str, run_id: str) -> dict[str, Any]:
49	    """Failure event appended to state for Agent Evolution monitoring."""
50	    return {
51	        "event_type": "gate_rejection",
52	        "gate_id": gate_id,
53	        "rationale": rationale,
54	        "run_id": run_id,
55	        "timestamp_utc": datetime.datetime.now(datetime.timezone.utc).isoformat(),
56	    }
57	
58	
59	_VALID_DECISIONS = {"approved", "rejected", "deferred"}
60	# Only 'approved' is terminal — it stops the gate re-firing on re-entry (don't
61	# re-unlock). 'rejected' routes back for re-review and 'deferred' parks the run for
62	# the operator to re-trigger later; BOTH must re-fire the interrupt when the gate
63	# is re-entered, so neither is terminal (pass-3 #5).
64	_TERMINAL_DECISIONS = {"approved"}
65	
66	
67	def _parse_operator_response(resp: Any, gate_id: str) -> tuple[str, str]:
68	    """
69	    Validate the operator's interrupt() response and return (decision, rationale).
70	
71	    A malformed response must NOT be silently coerced to 'rejected' (that would
72	    let a non-dict or a typo'd decision masquerade as a real governance decision,
73	    #163/#180). Fail loud so the operator re-submits a well-formed response on the
74	    next resume.
75	    """
76	    if not isinstance(resp, dict):
77	        raise ValueError(
78	            f"{gate_id}: operator response must be a dict like "
79	            f"{{'decision': 'approved'|'rejected'|'deferred', 'rationale': '...'}}, "
80	            f"got {type(resp).__name__}."
81	        )
82	    decision = resp.get("decision")
83	    if decision not in _VALID_DECISIONS:
84	        raise ValueError(
85	            f"{gate_id}: operator response 'decision' must be one of "
86	            f"{sorted(_VALID_DECISIONS)}, got {decision!r}."
87	        )
88	    rationale = resp.get("rationale") or "No rationale provided."
89	    return decision, rationale
90	
91	
92	def _already_decided(state: HCGRCState, gate_id: str) -> bool:
93	    """
94	    True if this gate already holds a TERMINAL decision (approved only).
95	
96	    Guards against a re-invocation re-firing the interrupt and re-running
97	    downstream effects on top of a terminal decision (#177). A 'rejected' record
98	    is deliberately NOT terminal: the Gate-2 reject → revise → re-review loop
99	    (route_after_gate_2 → exploratory → … → gate_2) must re-fire the interrupt so
100	    the operator is re-prompted, otherwise the gate short-circuits to {} and the
101	    graph spins until GraphRecursionError (pass-2 #184/#187). The loop-back path
102	    is responsible for producing the revised state that warrants re-review.
103	    """
104	    record = state.get("gate_status", {}).get(gate_id)
105	    return bool(record) and record.get("decision") in _TERMINAL_DECISIONS
106	
107	
108	def _finalize_gate(state: HCGRCState, gate_id: str, decision: str,
109	                   rationale: str, reviewer: str = "operator") -> dict[str, Any]:
110	    """Build the gate node's state update: failure event (if not approved) + the
111	    authoritative gate_status record (written only by gate_coordinator_node)."""
112	    update: dict[str, Any] = {}
113	    if decision == "rejected":
114	        # 'deferred' is a park, not a failure — only a rejection is a failure_event
115	        # (a deferred decision recorded as a rejection mislabels the audit trail).
116	        update["failure_events"] = [_rejection_event(gate_id, rationale, state["run_id"])]
117	    from .gate_coordinator import gate_coordinator_node
118	    update.update(gate_coordinator_node(state, gate_id, decision, rationale, reviewer=reviewer))
119	    return update
120	
121	
122	def _run_gate(state: HCGRCState, gate_id: str, proposal: dict[str, Any]) -> dict[str, Any]:
123	    """
124	    Drive one gate: idempotency guard, then interrupt() the operator and finalize.
125	
126	    Validation runs INSIDE a re-prompt loop (pass-3 #3): interrupt() consumes and
127	    persists the resume value before validation, so raising on a malformed value
128	    would strand it in the checkpoint and replay it forever. Instead, a malformed
129	    or mis-correlated response re-fires a fresh interrupt (next resume index)
130	    carrying the error, so the operator can re-submit and actually recover.
131	
132	    Gate-id correlation (pass-3 #4): if the operator response names a gate, it must
133	    match this gate — a stale/misrouted decision cannot be applied to the wrong gate.
134	    """
135	    if _already_decided(state, gate_id):
136	        return {}
137	    current = proposal
138	    while True:
139	        resp = interrupt(current)
140	        if isinstance(resp, dict) and resp.get("gate_id") not in (None, gate_id):
141	            current = {**proposal, "error": (
142	                f"response gate_id {resp.get('gate_id')!r} does not match {gate_id}")}
143	            continue
144	        try:
145	            decision, rationale = _parse_operator_response(resp, gate_id)
146	        except ValueError as exc:
147	            current = {**proposal, "error": str(exc)}
148	            continue
149	        return _finalize_gate(state, gate_id, decision, rationale)

content.content:
1	# ADR-0015: Architecture Integrity Decisions
2	
3	**Status:** Accepted  
4	**Date:** 2026-06-10  
5	**Author:** Thomas Jones  
6	**Supersedes:** N/A  
7	**Relates to:** ADR-0011 (resilience), ADR-0012 (autonomous architecture), ADR-0014 (infrastructure)
8	
9	---
10	
11	## Context
12	
13	Adversarial review surfaced ten architecture integrity issues (#71–#80) spanning: structural firewall implementation, orchestrator failure model, state schema safety, data split integrity, checkpoint scale, agent evolution scope, inter-agent trust, observability correlation, and gate delivery mechanism. This ADR captures the resolution of all ten.
14	
15	---
16	
17	## Decisions
18	
19	### 1. Structural Firewall Claims Are Conditional on Phase 0 (#71)
20	
21	All claims that "the firewall between phases is structural, not procedural" — in ADR-0004, ADR-0007, README, and the Charter — are conditional on Phase 0 implementation completing before any data acquisition.
22	
23	**Phase 0 success criteria (hard prerequisites, added to GATES.md):**
24	- LangGraph skeleton running with all gate nodes present
25	- Gate 1 firing with synthetic payload confirmed
26	- PostgresSaver checkpointing confirmed on target hardware
27	- Gate 2 data split logic implemented and idempotency test passing
28	- No SCF data acquired until all criteria are met
29	
30	The structural firewall claim becomes true when Phase 0 completes. Not before.
31	
32	---
33	
34	### 2. Orchestrator Failure Model — Option B + Evolve Layer (#72)
35	
36	**Option B — Defensive gate nodes:** Each gate validates its own preconditions and rejects invalid state regardless of how T-00 routed. Gate nodes are the enforcement surface.
37	
38	**Evolve layer:** Gate rejection events are logged with full context (proposed action, rejection reason, state snapshot, run_id, timestamp) to a `failure_events` store. Agent Evolution monitors `failure_events` for patterns:
39	
40	- **Routing errors** (T-00 operational): Agent Evolution applies DSPy re-optimization to T-00's routing prompt autonomously. Logged to PROV-DM with before/after versions.
41	- **Architectural failures** (gate topology wrong, systematic design flaw): Escalation filed to operator.
42	
43	This makes the platform self-correcting for operational routing failures without requiring a permanent watchdog node. Option A (real-time watchdog) becomes redundant once T-00's routing stabilizes through the evolve loop.
44	
45	**Protected agent set:** Agent Evolution may not autonomously modify P1–P5, statistical-analyst, or hypothesis-formalizer prompts. See Decision 6.
46	
47	---
48	
49	### 3. Provenance Entry Ordering (#73)
50	
51	Every entry appended to `prov_trace`, `errors`, and `null_results` carries:
52	- `timestamp`: ISO 8601 UTC, generated at write time by the writing agent
53	- `source_id`: agent name + run_id + superstep number
54	
55	Downstream consumers sort by `timestamp` before processing. List position is never used as a proxy for execution order. Enforced by ARA Rigor Reviewer at validation time.
56	
57	---
58	
59	### 4. Gate Status Write Protection (#74)
60	
61	All `gate_status` writes go through `gate_coordinator_node`. No other node writes this key directly.
62	
63	**Phase 0 (linear topology) — implemented via merge reducer:**
64	`gate_status` is typed `Annotated[dict[GateID, GateStatus], lambda a, b: {**a, **b}]`. Each `gate_coordinator_node` call returns only the new `{gate_id: record}` entry; LangGraph merges it into the existing dict. This is safe under concurrent writes because dict-merge is commutative for non-overlapping keys (each gate writes a unique `gate_id`).
65	
66	`gate_coordinator_node` is a helper function called from within gate nodes — it is **not** a standalone LangGraph node in Phase 0. The ADR was written before implementation; the merge reducer approach was selected during adversarial review (F1/F4, 2026-06-11) as the correct Phase 0 mechanism.
67	
68	**Phase 1 (parallel topology) — to be implemented:**
69	When Phase 1 introduces parallel agent execution, the merge reducer alone cannot prevent two agents writing the same `gate_id` key simultaneously. Phase 1 will promote `gate_coordinator_node` to a true serialized LangGraph node with explicit edges from all gate-writing agents. Tracked in GitHub issue #103.
70
